# Applications

## 1. Predicting the arrival of waves generated by ocean storms

**Real physical context**: storms in the Atlantic Ocean generate a broad spectrum of wavelengths that travel thousands of kilometres to European coasts. Early-warning systems for surfers and civil protection model the propagation of these waves using the gravity dispersion relation.

**Dominant hypothesis or simplification**: deep water throughout the propagation route; small amplitudes (linear waves); no amplitude dispersion due to ocean friction in the first approximation.

Dominant variable: [[lambda]], which determines [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] and therefore the arrival time of each spectral component. Longer waves arrive first because their [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] is greater. The temporal separation between the arrival of different [[lambda]] allows estimating the distance of the source.

**Physical interpretation**: each harmonic component of [[lambda]] travels at [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] proportional to the square root of [[lambda]]. A wave packet disperses: long components arrive days before short ones. Experienced surfers recognise the arrival of regular "swells" (long, gentle) before chaotic short waves.

Validity limit: the model fails when waves enter shallow water (continental shelf, less than 200 m depth for waves of hundreds of metres wavelength) or when amplitudes become comparable to the local depth. In those areas, the shallow-water correction with the hyperbolic tangent function is required.

---

## 2. Measuring surface tension by capillary wave analysis

**Real physical context**: in chemistry and surface physics laboratories, [[sigma]] of aqueous solutions, oils or biological liquids is measured by analysing the frequency and [[lambda]] of capillary waves generated by controlled surface vibration. This technique, called capillary wave tensiometry, is non-invasive and highly accurate.

**Dominant hypothesis or simplification**: pure capillary regime; very small amplitude (less than one hundredth of [[lambda]]); constant temperature; Newtonian fluid without viscoelasticity.

Dominant variable: [[sigma]], which directly sets [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] in the capillary regime given known [[numero_de_onda]] and [[rho]]. By measuring [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] and [[numero_de_onda]] experimentally, [[sigma]] is obtained by rearranging the capillary formula.

**Physical interpretation**: the capillary formula acts as an inverse equation linking the experimental observables ([[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] and [[numero_de_onda]]) to the desired material property ([[sigma]]). Surfactants, proteins or contaminants at the interface modify [[sigma]] detectably with 1% precision.

Validity limit: the method loses sensitivity for very thin contaminant layers (monomolecular) where the reduction in [[sigma]] is less than 1%. It also fails if the contaminant forms a rigid viscoelastic film rather than simply reducing [[sigma]]; in that case a more complex theory of waves at viscoelastic interfaces is required.

---

## 3. Design of coastal protection structures (breakwaters and seawalls)

**Real physical context**: coastal engineers design breakwaters and seawalls to protect harbours and beaches from wave erosion. The dimensioning of these structures requires knowing [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]], the wavelength of design waves and the local depth, all derived from the dispersion relation.

**Dominant hypothesis or simplification**: gravity regime for ocean waves with [[lambda]] of tens to hundreds of metres; shallow-water conditions in the structure zone (depths of 5 to 20 m); wave spectrum given by the meteorological statistics of the site.

Dominant variable: [[lambda]], which determines whether the structure acts as an effective barrier. A structure much shorter than [[lambda]] barely disperses the wave; to efficiently dissipate energy, the structure dimension must be comparable to [[lambda]].

**Physical interpretation**: waves with larger [[lambda]] have higher [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] and transport more energy per unit time. Protection design aims to reduce the [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] of waves reaching the coast by dissipating energy through reflection, diffraction and viscous dissipation in the structure material.

Validity limit: linear deep-water models are insufficient; waves near a breakwater are in the shallow-water, large-amplitude regime. Numerical finite-element or finite-volume models are required that incorporate the full dispersion relation and bottom interaction.

---

## 4. Formation of patterns on a rainfall surface (impact capillary waves)

**Real physical context**: when a raindrop impacts a calm water surface, it generates a ring of capillary waves that expands radially. The ring pattern carries information about [[sigma]], [[rho]] and the impact energy. This phenomenon is studied in meteorology and rain physics.

**Dominant hypothesis or simplification**: two-dimensional circular wave that can be locally analysed as a plane wave of given [[lambda]]; capillary regime for rings of smaller radius (centimetres and below); deep water relative to the [[lambda]] of the capillaries.

Dominant variable: [[sigma]], which controls the [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] of capillary rings of small radius. Higher [[sigma]] produces rings that expand faster and are more stable against viscous damping.

**Physical interpretation**: rings closest to the impact point correspond to short wavelengths (capillaries) and travel relatively fast. More distant rings, generated by larger [[lambda]], correspond to the gravity regime and travel more slowly. The resulting pattern shows inverse temporal dispersion for the two families of rings.

Validity limit: the simple capillary model fails in the moments immediately after impact, where the geometry is three-dimensional and the amplitude is large. Turbulence and splashing require nonlinear models. For radii greater than about 10 cm, the gravity regime begins to dominate.

---

## 5. Detection of contaminant films on the water surface (capillary sensors)

**Real physical context**: the presence of petroleum residues, oils or surfactants on the water surface reduces [[sigma]] measurably. Optical and acoustic sensors measure the [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] of artificially generated capillary waves to estimate the degree of surface contamination in real time.

**Dominant hypothesis or simplification**: pure capillary regime; [[lambda]] of the order of millimetres to maximise sensitivity to changes in [[sigma]]; controlled temperature to avoid thermal variations in [[sigma]].

Dominant variable: [[sigma]], whose reduction by contaminants decreases capillary [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]]. Sensitivity is maximum in the pure capillary regime: [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] is proportional to the square root of [[sigma]], so a 50% reduction in [[sigma]] produces a 29% reduction in [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]], detectable with acoustic precision.

**Physical interpretation**: the sensor emits a capillary wave of known [[numero_de_onda]] and measures the arrival time at the receiver. The measured [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]] is inverted in the capillary formula to obtain the effective [[sigma]] of the surface. Any contaminant reducing [[sigma]] manifests as a decrease in measured [[rapidez_de_propagacion_de_ondas_superficiales]]. Combined with [[rho]] measurements, one can estimate both contaminant concentration and type.

Validity limit: the method requires [[rho]] to be independently known, since the capillary formula involves both [[sigma]] and [[rho]]. For dense oils floating on water, [[rho]] of the mixed surface layer differs from pure water [[rho]], introducing additional uncertainty if not corrected. Temperature gradients also affect [[sigma]] and must be controlled to achieve precision better than 5%.
